fuzzy_prd:
We need a toolkit that automatically generates type-safe bindings for calling dynamically typed Python functions from a statically typed host language. Developers currently have to hand-write interop wrappers for every Python function they want to call, which is tedious and error-prone. The tool should read Python source text, extract every function definition header (including multi-line headers and those with trailing comments), and produce a ready-to-use method signature in the host language for each function found. The function name should be converted to the host language's upper-camel naming style, each parameter name should follow the lower-camel convention, and reserved words in the target language must be automatically escaped with a prefix character so the generated code compiles without modification. Type annotations should be translated to the closest strongly-typed equivalent in the host language, with well-known collection types mapping to idiomatic generic interfaces, tuple types handled specially depending on how many elements they carry, and anything unrecognized falling back to a generic dynamic object type. Default values must be preserved in the generated signature using the host language's own literal syntax, including numeric bases for hex and binary literals. When a function header cannot be parsed, the tool must report the exact character position of the failure rather than silently skipping it. The error reporting approach should follow the same pattern as the identifier validation module. The toolkit must also be usable as a standalone command-line adapter that accepts a JSON command on standard input and writes plain text to standard output, with all error conditions reported as neutral category lines rather than raw runtime exceptions.

One small follow-up from the questions that came in: for tuple annotations, `tuple[T]` should come through as `ValueTuple<mapped(T)>`, not a parenthesized tuple. The parenthesized comma-separated form `(mapped(T1),mapped(T2),...)` is only for tuples with two or more elements. Also, any unrecognized scalar name like `object`, `Any`, or unknown custom types should map to `PyObject`, and the same rule applies to any unknown generic head too, including when that shows up inside nested generic arguments.

On the parameter output shape, the `default=<value>` line should only be emitted when the parameter actually has a default, meaning `DefaultValue != null`. If there is no default, then the output is exactly three lines: `name=<name>`, `type=<type>`, `annotated=<true|false>`. In other words, never emit a `default=` line for a parameter with no default.

A couple command behavior specifics too: if the `action` field in the JSON input doesn’t match any known action string, the output is exactly `error=unknown_action\n`. If something uncaught escapes at the top level, that should become `error=internal\n`. And for normal action-level parse issues, keep returning the action-specific error lines like `error=invalid_type\n`, `error=invalid_signature\n`, `error=invalid_parameter\n`, `error=invalid_parameter_list\n`, `error=invalid_function\n`, `error=not_an_integer\n`.

Last thing on parameter kinds: `**kwargs` should map to `IReadOnlyDictionary<string, PyObject>`. `*args` should map to `ValueTuple<PyObject>` and in the generated signature it is always named `args`. Same goes for a bare keyword-only separator `*` with no name after it — that should introduce the same `ValueTuple<PyObject> args` placeholder in the generated signature as a real `*args` variadic parameter would.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: public static bool TryParseFunctionDefinitions(SourceText source, out PythonFunctionDefinition[] pythonSignatures, out GeneratorError[] errors)

trigger_keywords: what happens when return annotation is None
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A return annotation of `None` produces `void` as the return type in the generated method signature. A completely absent return annotation produces `PyObject` (the dynamic placeholder).

trigger_keywords: how does pascal case handle segments with one character
empty segment in snake case conversion
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In `ToPascalCase`, a segment produced by splitting on `_` that has fewer than 2 characters (i.e., length < 2, meaning length 0 or 1) contributes a literal underscore `_` to the output, not the character itself uppercased. Only segments with at least 2 characters have their first character uppercased. This means `hello__world` gives `Hello_World` (double underscore yields one underscore) and a leading underscore is preserved.

trigger_keywords: how are reserved keywords escaped in parameter names
what prefix is used for keyword collision
parameter name collision with host language keyword
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a parameter name after camelCase conversion matches a C# reserved keyword (checked via `Keywords.IsKeyword()`), it is prefixed with `@` (at-sign). For example, a Python parameter named `in` becomes `@in` in the generated signature.

trigger_keywords: how are hex integer defaults rendered
binary integer literal in default value
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Hexadecimal integer defaults are rendered as `0x` followed by uppercased hex digits (e.g., `0xDEADBEEF`). Binary integer defaults are rendered as `0b` followed by the binary representation of the value. The format string used is `0x{0:X}` for hex and `0b{binary}` for binary.

Parse errors are reported with zero-based line and column indices. A `GeneratorError` carries `StartLine`, `StartColumn`, `EndLine`, `EndColumn` (all zero-based). The error format in the text output is `error <StartLine>:<StartColumn>-<EndLine>:<EndColumn>` with no spaces around the dash.

`dict[K, V]` maps to `IReadOnlyDictionary<mapped(K),mapped(V)>`. The generic head is matched case-insensitively, so `Dict[K, V]` behaves identically. The output is compact with no spaces after commas.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the same pattern as the identifier validation module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The `Keywords.cs` file containing `Keywords.IsKeyword()` and `Keywords.ValidIdentifier()` which checks against the C# reserved keyword list and applies the `@` prefix escape. The error reporting approach (returning structured error objects with position info) mirrors how `GeneratorError` is used throughout the parser.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that constraint. Please check the source files in the PythonSourceGenerator project, particularly the Parser and Reflection subdirectories, for the exact implementation details.

feature1_1_pascal_case.json.description: Convert a snake_case style identifier into a PascalCase identifier suitable for a statically typed target language. The identifier is split on underscore boundaries; each non-empty segment has its first character upper-cased and the remainder preserved, then the segments are concatenated. A trailing underscore (an empty final segment) and runs of consecutive underscores are preserved as literal underscore characters in the output. A leading underscore is likewise preserved. Output is the converted identifier followed by a trailing newline.

feature1_2_camel_case.json.description: Convert a snake_case style identifier into a camelCase identifier (lower-cased leading character) suitable for a statically typed target language. The conversion first builds the PascalCase form (split on underscores, capitalize each non-empty segment, preserve empty segments and leading underscores as literal underscores), then forces the very first character of the result to lower-case. Note that for an input that begins with an underscore the first character is already an underscore, so the output is identical to the PascalCase form. Output is the converted identifier followed by a trailing newline.

feature2_type_mapping.json.description: Map a Python type annotation (given as its textual form) to the equivalent type in a statically typed target language. Scalar builtins map to their target primitives. A homogeneous sequence type maps to a generic enumerable of the mapped element type. A fixed-size heterogeneous sequence with two or more elements maps to a target tuple type written as a parenthesized, comma-separated list of mapped element types; a single-element fixed sequence maps to the explicit one-arity value-tuple generic. A mapping/association type maps to a read-only dictionary generic over its mapped key and value types. The generic head name is matched case-insensitively, so capitalized aliases behave identically to their lower-case forms. An unknown element type, or an unrecognized type name, maps to the opaque dynamic object type. Element types nest recursively. Output is the rendered target type (compact form, no spaces after commas) followed by a trailing newline.

feature3_signature_generation.json.description: Generate a target-language instance method signature from a single Python function definition supplied as source text. The Python function name is converted to PascalCase to form the method name. The return annotation is mapped to the target type, with the special none-return annotation producing a void method. Each parameter is rendered as its mapped target type followed by its name converted to lower-camelCase; a parameter whose name collides with a reserved keyword of the target language is escaped with a leading at-sign. A variadic positional parameter is modeled as a one-arity value-tuple of the opaque dynamic object type and is always named with the conventional variadic name; a variadic keyword parameter is modeled as a read-only dictionary from string to the opaque dynamic object type. A bare keyword-only separator introduces the same variadic-positional placeholder. Parameter default values are rendered idiomatically: integers and floats as numeric literals, booleans as the target boolean literals, the none constant as the target null literal, and hexadecimal / binary integer literals preserved in their original base. Output is the rendered signature (return type, a space, method name, then the parenthesized parameter list with a space after each comma) followed by a trailing newline.

cases.expected_output: void HelloWorld(string a, ValueTuple<PyObject> args, IReadOnlyDictionary<string, PyObject> kwargs)

feature4_1_parse_parameter.json.description: Parse a single Python function parameter from its textual form and report its structural components. A parameter has a name, an optional type annotation, and an optional default value. When a type annotation is present its rendered form is reported and the annotation flag is true; when absent the type defaults to the dynamic-any placeholder and the annotation flag is false. A default value, when present, is reported in its canonical textual form (numbers as written, quoted strings with the quotes stripped, the boolean and none constants by name). Output reports, one per line: name, type, the annotated flag (true/false), and, only when a default exists, the default value.

feature4_2_parse_parameter_list.json.description: Parse a parenthesized Python parameter list and report each parameter in order. The list is delimited by parentheses and parameters are comma-separated. Each parameter may carry a type annotation; a parameter without an annotation is reported with the dynamic-any placeholder type. Output is one line per parameter, in declaration order, each formatted as the parameter name, a colon, and the rendered type.

feature4_3_parse_function.json.description: Parse a single-line Python function definition header (the def keyword, the function name, a parameter list, an optional return annotation, and the trailing colon) and report its structure. Each parameter without a type annotation is reported with the dynamic-any placeholder type; a function without a return annotation is reported with the dynamic-any return type. Output reports the function name, then one line per parameter (in order) giving the parameter name and its rendered type, then the rendered return type.

input.text: def foo(a: int, b: str) -> None:

feature4_4_parse_module.json.description: Scan a complete block of Python source text and extract every top-level function definition, ignoring all non-function lines (imports, assignments, guard blocks, bodies). Function headers that span multiple physical lines, headers that carry a trailing inline comment after the colon, headers with trailing whitespace after the colon, and a final header that is not followed by a blank line must all be recognized. Output first reports the count of recognized functions, then one line per function (in source order) rendering the function header as the name, a parenthesized comma-separated list of name:type parameters, an arrow, and the return type; then it reports the count of parse errors, followed by one line per error.

cases.expected_output:
functions=2
def bar(a:int, b:str) -> None
def baz(c:float, d:bool) -> None
errors=0

feature4_5_parse_errors.json.description: When a function header inside a source block is malformed, the scan reports a parse error that pinpoints where tokenization broke down rather than silently skipping or crashing. The error carries a start and end position, each expressed as a zero-based line index and a column index, so a tool can underline the offending span. Output reports the count of recognized functions, then the count of errors, then one line per error giving the start line and column and the end line and column of the offending span.

input.source:
def bar(a: int, b:= str) -> None:
    pass

feature5_integer_literals.json.description: Parse a Python integer literal from its textual form and report its decimal value. Plain decimal literals may carry an optional leading minus sign. Hexadecimal literals use the 0x prefix and binary literals use the 0b prefix; both are unsigned and are decoded to their numeric value. Output is the integer value rendered in base ten, followed by a trailing newline.
